name: Jimmy Forrest
abstract: James Robert Forrest Jr. (January 24, 1920 – August 26, 1980) was an American jazz musician who played tenor saxophone throughout his career.

concerts.label: Newport Jazz Festival: Count Basie and His Orchestra
Newport Jazz Festival: Count Basie and His Orchestra
Billy Eckstine with Count Basie and His Orchestra
Newport Jazz Festival: Count Basie and His Orchestra

about_quotes.speaker: Elvin Jones
about_quotes.evidence: Tommy Potter initially, Jimmy Forrest, Tommy Flanagan and myself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Elvin Jones — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Harry Edison
about_quotes.evidence: I had Elvin Jones, Tommy Flanagan, Jimmy Forrest, and Joe Benjamin. I had a real good swinging band too... I kept that band together for about six years.
about_quotes.doc_title: Harry Edison — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jay McShann
about_quotes.evidence: I can remember when, you know Jimmy Forrest was a great player. He was on that same band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jay McShann interviewed by Monk Rowe, Sarasota, Florida, April 12, 1996

about_quotes.speaker: Terry Clark
about_quotes.evidence: So he said, “Get the kid.” I guess he figured he wasn’t going to have to pay him much money, as much money anyhow as Forrest. So I got Bob Graf to come join Basie’s band... We got two cats here who play very well. One is an old, established brother named Jimmy Forrest
about_quotes.doc_title: Terry Clark — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Red Callender
about_quotes.evidence: Jack Lauderdale and I get into his Cadillac, and drive back to St. Louis to listen to Jimmy Forrest. So Jimmy Forrest played... I said, 'Hey, let's record this guy.'

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Foster
about_quotes.evidence: the other tenor saxophone chair that was ultimately occupied by Eddie “Lockjaw” Davis, Eric Dixon, Jimmy Forrest, even Oliver Nelson at one time, and Harold Ashby
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Foster — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
